title: Gods of Pegana, by Lord Dunsany - Dungeon Dive Book Club review
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=c2ket6Qczzw

The Book Club's September review, read from the Ballantine Adult Fantasy edition — which gives Daniel (@dungeondive) his opening theme: a tribute to Lin Carter, whose 1970s reprint series rescued out-of-print fantasy for a post-Tolkien readership and whose introductions remain masterclasses in genre history. The Gods of Pegāna itself he classifies as cosmological fiction — kin to the Silmarillion and Genesis — a creation story rather than a narrative. He retells its central conceit: MANA-YOOD-SUSHAI, the sleeping creator, dreams on while the drummer Skarl plays; should the drum ever stop, everything ends. From that image flow thirty miniature myths of small gods, prophets, and the god of little dreams, Yoharneth-Lahai, whose passage Daniel reads aloud in full King James cadence. His honest counsel: this is a slow, careful read despite its eighty pages — closer to studying scripture or Shakespeare than consuming a plot. There is no dramatic tension to summarise; it's a tone poem, and its influence runs straight to Lovecraft's Azathoth and Clark Ashton Smith's worlds. The joy is in the language itself, and in feeling like you're holding a religious text from an antiquity that never was. Discussion scheduled for September 25th.

----

Dunsany wrote a whole cosmology with no plot and no protagonist. What's the best book you've loved purely for its language and world, with barely a story in sight?
